mission.title: Install and Configure Cilium on Kubernetes
mission.description: Cilium is an eBPF-based networking, security, and observability solution for Kubernetes. It provides advanced networking capabilities and security policies, making it ideal for modern cloud-native applications.

steps.title: Add the Cilium Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Cilium Helm repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add cilium https://helm.cilium.io/
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Cilium using Helm
steps.description:
Install Cilium in the 'cilium-system' namespace with the specified version:
```bash
helm install cilium cilium/cilium --version v1.19.1 --namespace cilium-system --create-namespace
```

steps.title: Verify the Cilium installation
steps.description:
Check that the Cilium pods are running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n cilium-system
```

steps.title: Set up Cilium network policies
steps.description:
Create a simple network policy to restrict traffic:
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
EOF
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Cilium will show the Cilium pods running in the 'cilium-system' namespace. You can also verify that the network policies are enforced by testing connectivity between pods.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add cilium https://helm.cilium.io/
helm repo update
helm install cilium cilium/cilium --version v1.19.1 --namespace cilium-system --create-namespace

kubectl edit deployment cilium --namespace cilium-system
# Add the following under spec.template.spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"

cat <<EOF | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-all
  namespace: default
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
EOF

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Cilium release from the 'cilium-system' namespace using Helm. This command will remove all resources associated with the release.
```bash
helm uninstall cilium --namespace cilium-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were created during the installation of Cilium. This command will delete the CRDs associated with Cilium.
```bash
kubectl delete crd ciliumnetworkpolicies.cilium.io
kubectl delete crd ciliumnetworkendpoints.cilium.io
kubectl delete crd ciliumendpoints.cilium.io
kubectl delete crd ciliumnodes.cilium.io
kubectl delete crd ciliumclusterwidenetworkpolicies.cilium.io
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'cilium-system' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed successfully.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace cilium-system
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, it's a good practice to back up the current state of the Cilium installation. You can do this by exporting the current configuration and resources.
```bash
kubectl get all -n cilium-system -o yaml > cilium-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Cilium release to the latest version. Replace 'v1.19.1' with the desired version if needed.
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade cilium cilium/cilium --version v1.19.1 --namespace cilium-system
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
After upgrading, check that the Cilium pods are running the new version and are healthy.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n cilium-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Cilium pods not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Cilium pods are not starting, check the pod logs for errors. This can indicate issues with configuration or resource limits.
```bash
kubectl logs -n cilium-system <pod-name>
```

troubleshooting.title: Network policies not being enforced
troubleshooting.description:
If network policies are not being enforced, verify that the Cilium agent is running and check the network policy configuration.
```bash
kubectl get networkpolicy -n default
kubectl get pods -n cilium-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Cilium not connecting to Kubernetes API
troubleshooting.description:
If Cilium cannot connect to the Kubernetes API, check the Cilium pod logs for connection errors and ensure that the service account has the necessary permissions.
```bash
kubectl logs -n cilium-system <pod-name>
kubectl describe clusterrole cilium
```

troubleshooting.title: High CPU usage by Cilium pods
troubleshooting.description:
If you notice high CPU usage by Cilium pods, review the resource limits set in the deployment and adjust them if necessary. You can also check the metrics for more details.
```bash
kubectl top pods -n cilium-system
kubectl edit deployment cilium --namespace cilium-system
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
